name: [kbatch] completions dropped — batch count loss / stuck batches
message:
One or more job completion events could not be applied to their batch (reason: not_found/invalid). The count is permanently lost, so the batch will never reach total and gets stuck below completion.

Most common cause: the daemon reads a **different Redis** than the producer that created the batch, or a mismatched **topic_prefix / events_topic** between worker and daemon; also a batch whose TTL expired.

Runbook:
1. Check daemon logs for `[kbatch-event] DROPPED completion reason=...` (includes batch_id/batch_seq/job_id).
2. Verify brokers / redis_url / topic_prefix / events_topic match across producer, worker, and daemon.
3. Inspect the batch: `HGETALL kafka_batch:b:<batch_id>` — expect completed+failed < total.

{{#is_alert}}Dropped completions detected in the last 5m.{{/is_alert}}
@CHANGE_ME

options.escalation_message: Still dropping completions — batches are accumulating in a stuck state.

name: [kbatch] callback produce failed — parked on dead-letter
message:
A completed batch's callback could not be produced to the callbacks topic after retries and was parked on the dead-letter topic (dlt_type=callback_produce_failed). The batch finished correctly but its on_complete/on_success callback did not fire until replayed.

Runbook:
1. Check daemon logs for `[kbatch-event] callback produce failed batch_id=...`.
2. Confirm the callbacks topic is healthy (broker/partition availability).
3. Replay the parked callback payloads from the dead-letter topic once the topic recovers.

{{#is_alert}}Callback(s) dead-lettered in the last 5m.{{/is_alert}}
@CHANGE_ME

name: [kbatch] recurring schedule stale — not firing on cadence
message:
A recurring (cron) schedule has been idle longer than its staleness threshold (default 2x its own interval) — it is enabled but not firing. This is the 'the scheduler silently stopped for this schedule' alarm.

Runbook:
1. Check daemon logs for `[kbatch-cron] STALE schedule={{schedule.name}} ...`.
2. Confirm the recurring scheduler holds the leader lock and is ticking (see the heartbeat monitor).
3. Inspect the schedule: `./bin/schedule list` — check enabled, next_run, last_fire; verify the cron/timezone are valid.

{{#is_alert}}Schedule {{schedule.name}} is stale.{{/is_alert}}
@CHANGE_ME

name: [kbatch] recurring scheduler heartbeat missing — ticker stopped
[kbatch] batch push rejected — sealed/cancelled batch (create-sealed race)
[kbatch] workset payload missing — SuperFetch in-flight jobs LOST
message:
Reclaim found aged workset index entries whose job payload had already expired — those SuperFetch in-flight jobs are LOST (their Kafka offset was committed at claim, and there is no payload left to re-run). The batch will never reach total.

Root cause is almost always super_fetch_lease_ttl set at/below liveness_ttl + orphan_grace + reclaim_interval, so a crashed pod's payload expires before reclaim is allowed to touch it. (The worker now raises the effective lease above that floor and logs a warning; a non-zero value here means the floor is still being crossed or reclaim was down for longer than the lease.)

Runbook:
1. Check worker logs for `[kbatch-workset] list_orphans: N aged index entries missing job payloads`.
2. Ensure super_fetch_lease_ttl comfortably exceeds liveness_ttl + orphan_grace + reclaim_interval.
3. Ensure a reclaimer (worker or daemon) is always running.

{{#is_alert}}Workset payloads missing in the last 5m — jobs lost.{{/is_alert}}
@CHANGE_ME

options.escalation_message: Still losing SuperFetch in-flight jobs to expired payloads.

name: [kbatch] workset payload unreclaimable — dead-lettered
